caption:
The final Technique of the Year: Dynos!

Sometimes the best beta is leaving the wall. Dynos challenge your power, coordination, and confidence, perfect for learning when to trust the jump instead of forcing a static move.

Quick tips:
1️⃣ Squish the bug to set your feet
2️⃣ Keep your eyes on the prize
3️⃣ Push up with your legs
4️⃣ Follow through with your arms to launch

2️⃣ VO₂ Max Training — 4 minutes of consistent effort, 4 minutes off, for 4 rounds.

author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
Good bolts brought to you by the @asca_bolt_replacement 😎🔩

Since 1998, the ASCA has supplied over 80,000 bolts and 42,000 anchors for replacement work, along with lower-offs, tools, and other essential gear. Born in Yosemite, their efforts began on classic big-wall routes in the West and quickly expanded to crags across the country. The ASCA is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that relies entirely on support from the climbing community.

How can you support? 🤝💪

Join us at Belmont for our 7th annual 12-hour endurance Battle for Bolts to raise funds for the ASCA — and this year, Movement is matching donations up to $5,000!

Expect contests, mini-games (yes, including a table traverse) 🎯, a special-edition Battle for Bolts mug and tote while supplies last 🥤👜, plus prizes from @thenorthface, @sterlingrope, @yyvertical_official, @wildcountry_official, @edelrid_sport, @willsewforbolts, @bespokecraftdesign, @prana, and more.

We’re also hosting a silent auction 🫰, including a chance to win an amazing crash pad from @dragmeupthewall x @organicclimbing! 🧗‍♂️

➡️ sign up through link in our bio!

A big thank you to Jay, from @willsewforbolts for showing us what Good Bolts look like vs. Bad Bolts.

author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
🧗 New Month, New Technique: Laybacks
 
A layback is all about opposition: pulling with your hands while pressing your feet against the wall or feature to create tension. It’s a go-to move for cracks, aretes, and flakes where there’s nothing good to stand on straight-on. 💥
 
How to Use It
• Pull with your hands while pushing with your feet
• Keep your hips close to the wall for balance
• Move smoothly, matching hands and feet as you climb
 
🎯 Pro Tip: Keep your core engaged and your feet active—the layback only works if both are working together.

As always, peep the class calendar in our bio for technique classes!
author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
Tip #3: Pack it out—yes, all of it 🚮✨

In Part 3 of our bouldering etiquette series, Yosemite Climbing Rangers (@climber_stewards) Gus & Ethan highlight two big issues: micro trash and human waste.

🗑️ Even with the best intentions and Leave No Trace (LNT) practices, little bits of trash (tape, wrappers, chalk chunks) get left behind. Help keep Yosemite clean by picking up any litter you find, even if it’s not yours. It’s a simple act that makes a big difference (and earns you some good karma).

🚫💩 Sadly, human waste is an ongoing issue in Yosemite, often due to hikers mistakenly thinking a Boulder area offers privacy. Few things kill motivation like finding a surprise in the landing zone. Keep a few doggy bags and gloves in your car so you can clean up if needed, ensuring a better experience for yourself and others! 

🌲 Meet the Yosemite Climbing Rangers 🌲
These dedicated stewards protect Yosemite’s climbing areas, share education, and host community stewardship events. Follow their work at @climber_stewards.

author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
Tip #2: The tick fairy isn’t real—clean up your own tick marks 🧚‍♂️❌ 

In Part 2 of the bouldering etiquette series, Movement Ambassador @nikscaryberry and the Yosemite Climbing Rangers @climber_stewards Gus and Ethan remind us: 

When approaching a boulder problem, there are generally two types of experiences: discovering all the beta on your own, or receiving beta from others. Leaving tick marks can influence which experience the next climber has by offering visual clues. While both approaches are valid, our actions should not dictate someone else’s journey on a problem. Additionally, tick marks detract from the natural beauty of Yosemite’s boulders. Please brush off all tick marks and excessive chalk from holds before you leave.

🌲 Meet the Yosemite Climbing Rangers 🌲
These dedicated stewards protect Yosemite’s climbing areas, share education, and host community stewardship events. Follow their work at @climber_stewards.
author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
Tip #1: Be mindful of your crew size 👯‍♀️ 

Movement Ambassador Nik Berry (@nikscaryberry) teams up with Yosemite Climbing Rangers Ethan & Gus for Part 1 of their 3-part series on outdoor bouldering etiquette. Whether you’re new to the sport or a seasoned crusher, these tips will help you climb responsibly and keep the stoke high for everyone. 😎 

The Yosemite Valley Loop Trail runs close to many boulders—and in some cases, right by the landings of problems. Be mindful of your group size, avoid blocking trails with crash pads, and refrain from placing personal items on or near the trail. Larger groups can mean larger impacts, so please tread lightly. 

✨ About the Yosemite Climbing Rangers ✨ Dedicated stewards of Yosemite climbing areas, they educate climbers, protect the environment, and foster community through stewardship events. Learn more about their incredible work by following them on Instagram @climber_stewards

✨Shout out to the climbers: @leothelifeguardd @nicosancheznsz @charlie.hamilt0n @winonaspohr @kai_the_berg @oliveranunez05 @snowy_dry @jonamayaaa

caption:

If you climb in the South Bay, you’ve probably crossed paths with Sara! With 14+ years of climbing and 4 years of coaching, she brings an analytical yet personal approach to every session. Her background in math and engineering shapes her coaching style, combining precision with a genuine passion for helping climbers reach their full potential.

Sara coaches across multiple programs at Movement: she’s a Youth Competition Team coach, a private coach at Sunnyvale and Santa Clara, and leads the Advanced Peak Performance Team at Sunnyvale.

caption:
🧗 SF’s Technique of the Month: Stemming

This month, we’re dialing into a technique that thrives on corners, dihedrals, and chimneys: stemming. ✨

How to Use It
• Place your feet and/or hands against opposite surfaces
• Press outward and engage your legs to stay upright
• Keep your body in tension between the walls

🎯 Pro Tip: Focus on balance over muscle. Smooth, controlled movement wins.

Want to level up your skills? Check out our technique classes at all Bay Area gyms through the climbing class calendar linked in our bio!

author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
🧗‍♀️ SF’s Technique of the Month: Heel Hooks

We’re kicking off the month with one of climbing’s most versatile moves: the heel hook 💪

Heel hooks let you use the back of your foot to pull in, create tension, and stay stable on overhangs or big moves. They’re also great for slopey holds or holds that face away from you—places where your toes just won’t cut it. It’s a total game-changer for body control, balance, and reach!

🎯 Pro Tip: Use Your Whole Leg
• Place your heel above or beside the hold
• Pull with your leg to bring your hips and body close to the wall
• Engage your core and move with control

Climbing in SF? Look for TOTM stickers on bouldering and roped climbs designed to help you practice this technique. And don’t forget to check our climbing calendar for technique classes across our Bay Area gyms to level up your movement!

caption:
Beginner Climbing Tip: Use Your Toe Box! 

When you’re first learning to climb, it’s common to place your arch on footholds—but for better control, aim to use the toe box of your climbing shoe instead.
 
The toe box (the stiff front part of your shoe) is designed to give you:
✅ Precision on small footholds
✅ Better weight transfer and body positioning
✅ More mobility to pivot and turn your hips
 
Ready to build your skills? Join one of our Intro to Technique classes!

caption:
Sunnyvale Powerlifting Competition 💪
In May, our South Bay Fitness Manager Oscar hosted another incredible powerlifting competition at Sunnyvale, bringing together 14 strong, dedicated lifters from across the Bay Area.

Over half competed in the Open Women’s category, many of whom trained for months leading up to the event. The energy in the gym was electric ⚡️—cheers echoed as PRs were broken and first-time competitors found the confidence to keep going.

What stood out most was the community. New friendships formed, familiar faces returned, and support poured in from every corner of the gym, even from guests just stopping by.

Huge shoutout to the Sunnyvale front desk crew and the amazing volunteers who helped make it all happen!
author: movementgymsbayarea
movementgymsbayarea
movementgymsbayarea

caption:
🧗‍♂️ SF’s Technique of the Month: Toe Hooks 

This month is all about Toe hooks. Toe hooks let you hold tension, and can offer stability or reach in situations where other options won’t work. 

🎯 Pro Tips:
• Pull with the top of your foot like you would with your hands
• Keep tension through your leg and core
• Use it to match, move, or even rest

Climbing in SF? Look for TOTM stickers on roped and bouldering climbs to help you practice the technique.

Want more tips? Check out our blog and climbing calendar in the link in our bio for technique breakdowns and free member classes!
author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
Happy Pride Month! 🏳️‍🌈
 
Our 2025 Pride shirts are here, illustrated by Rockville, Maryland-based artist Helena Wu!

We’re donating $10 in proceeds from every shirt sold in June to our community partner @queercrushclimbing 
 
🌈About the artist:
Helena (she/her) @helena_pocket is a Rockville, Maryland-based freelance illustrator whose work is infused with dreamy colors and nature-inspired vibes. With a portfolio spanning video games, product design, and a variety of other freelance work, Helena also actively engages with the local (and queer) art scene, frequently tabling at markets throughout the Baltimore and the DMV region (DC, MD, VA).

Helena’s love for climbing started in 2016. She’s a boulderer at heart, but will always offer to clean up sport anchors just to spend a little longer on the wall. Always trying to merge her hobbies together, she previously worked on the Movement Rockville Murals in 2023. She loves finding new ways to subtly celebrate the queer community and explore gender expression, and this year, she was given the opportunity to create a fun, dynamic graphic to showcase the vibrancy of the queer climbing community!

author: movementgymsbayarea

caption:
🧗‍♀️ SF’s Technique of the Month: Mantles

Mantling is a technique where you press down on a hold and rock your weight over it—like getting out of a pool without a ladder. It’s all about body tension, balance, and trusting your feet to finish the move.

🎯 Pro Tip: Think Press & Shift
• Push down on the hold like it’s a countertop
• Step high and press through your foot
• Shift your weight over your hand to stand up

Climbing in SF? Look for TOTM stickers on roped and bouldering climbs to help you practice the technique!

Looking for more technique classes? Check out our climbing calendar for upcoming sessions across our Bay Area gyms!
author: movementgymsbayarea
